speaker: interviewer
text: Part eight of the interview with Liz Manning, March 8th, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandmother, my father's mother. Her name was Kathi. Later a little she lived in Lautenbach, which is a sub-- which was outside of Vienna, in the place where they had been living since [? 1500, ?] [? something, ?] the family. And this picture was taken sometime in the 1930s for a passport photograph. She's in her Sunday-- in her Shabbat best. She's wearing a Sheitel. The rest of-- the rest of her life, she always wore a scarf. She only wore that for special occasions. This is my father's first cousin. She was Mira's mother. The one that-- she-- she-- she died when her daughter was four years old. The reason this picture is included in this is because when I saw it for the first time, and a very large copy of it, I looked at it, and she had my face. When I look at her now, I still look at it, and I see myself when I was 20 years old in a funny hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Emma.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken sometimes before 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it taken in where she lived in Yugoslav?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have absolutely no idea. I do not know. This was taken during the First World War. It must have been in the beginning. Because later on, my father would have been in the army. The man in the middle is my father. The woman on the left of him, leaning out the window, is his younger sister, the one that wound up in Israel. And the one on the right, sitting down in front, is my Aunt [? Taeta, ?] the mother of us all, my father's older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just say their names one more time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one on the left is Adelle. My father's name was Deszo. And [? Taeta ?] was Berta, but everybody called her [? Taeta ?]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my Uncle Albert. His name was [? Abraham ?] [? Papa. ?] He was a wonderful man. There's something I would like to tell. My mother was a very-- that I didn't say before, but it's worthwhile so you will know him. He was a wonderful man. And he-- my mother was a very clean woman, and she liked to dress us up in silk suits, and linen dresses, and patent leather shoes, and something. And he would come every Sunday, pick up my brother, buy him the largest bar of chocolate he could find. And he had to eat it-- had to hold it in his hand for a half hour, until it was all soft. And then he-- when it was all soft, he let him eat it. And then he brought him home. And my mother would think he was a wonderful man. We all adored him. He was my Aunt [? Taeta's ?] husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken sometimes in the 1930s. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of me when I was four years old. I had very, very dark brown hair and black eyes. And today I have very light brown hair and green eyes. And a lot of other things have happened between. But the inside is still the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1930, when I was four years old. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my ID card from past 1938. You can see in the left-hand corner there, it says 'Evacuated'-- in-- in German, of course-- in November of 1943. That was stamped on my card so that I would not be able to exist any place outside of a concentration camp. This showed, in my identity card, that I was evacuated. Which meant I was sent away. You can also see that my name is Lizy [? Sarah, ?] because every Jewish female had to take the name [? Sarah. ?] That's about it. That's what I looked like. And this was taken about 1939. Yeah, [? German, ?] 13th of July, 1939. The date is in there. That's when we all had to assume the name of [? Sarah, ?] and we got identity cards with that addition in there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of me taken at one of those fast one-hour photograph places. I was about 15 years old at the time. No, I was 16. Because my mother was no longer alive when this picture was taken. It's in Vienna, before I was sent away. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is a picture taken of my brother Henry and myself-- I'm Liz-- in about 1940. I think this picture was taken a little bit earlier than the other one. And we have matching-- they were our pride and joy-- we had matching windbreakers at the time. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of my youth group. I belonged to a Jewish youth organization. And we would meet once a week. And all these people were all my age. The one that's standing, the second one from the left with the curly hair, was my very best friend. She had the most fabulously beautiful red hair that you can imagine. I am the one standing up, the taller one behind the seated person in the middle. And out of these people that are in this picture, I am the only one that was alive in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of your friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend's name was [? Rene ?] [? Shpieza ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the name of this organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the organization was Hashomer Hatzair. And the kvutza name was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of organization was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish youth. I don't know. We went and they-- for the-- in the youth aliyah, building. And we-- we were a Jewish group. We were a lot of girls. We talked about Israel. We talked about being Jewish. We talked about Jewish writers, Jewish philosophers. It was very-- it was a very worthwhile thing. Because none of us had an opportunity to go to school. And it was-- the one that was our leader was only three years older than we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you a member of this group? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I got into it at about 19, when I came back from the-- from the labor camp. And then, slowly but surely, the group dissolved itself, because everybody was sent away to be killed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my father. The reason he looks so worn and worried is that-- I just realized it, actually, today, when I took it out-- that there is a white string in is lapel. And that what is in there is his-- it's the tag, which was his ID thing, like an identity tag, for Theresienstadt. This is-- this was the picture when we-- that was taken of him either when-- before we went on the train, or after we came off it. And that's why he looks so-- he was usually smiling. But he didn't have much to smile about on that day. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the identity tag that I wore when I was taken to Theresienstadt. I was in the transport. My-- the-- the first thing was a Roman 4, which designated that I was in Vienna, that I came from Vienna. The 14 was that it was the 14th series of transport. N meant that it was not a 1,000 person transport, but it was alphabetically. It had about 100, 150-- as many as they could round up. I was 414 and 960. The only thing that is on there is the 960 number, which was my number. If I had been tattooed, that would have been the number they would have put on me. But they didn't tattoo in the place where I was. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of a place called the [INAUDIBLE], the Dresden Armory. This is the-- the one-- the building in the center on top of the street. It is the-- there was a women's barracks. And that was the first place I lived in. And actually, the story that I remembered about the liberation from the camp was behind this building, where the road passed. And this is the type of-- the one on the left-- of the type of barracks where we lived in stables and rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is a picture of Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the actual town of Theresienstadt, where civilians had lived, until they decided to take all the civilians out in 1940, and make a ghetto for the Jews, that they-- This is one of the food coupons that we were given in Theresienstadt. Each day has two coupons. One was for the evening soup. And one was for the main meal. In the morning, you could go and get this concoction that they called coffee as often as you wanted. But nobody that had any sense would drink it, because it was full of chemicals and it was not edible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was lucky. I was young enough, I could do without. Older people would sometimes stand there and ask you, are you taking the soup? And if you said no, they would get that gray gruel for-- for lunch. And then they would stand next to you and take your potato peelings. And they didn't live very long. But I guess when you're hungry, you're-- you're out of control. How have you manage to save this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saved this stuff because it was very important to me. It was part of my life, and I'm a very orderly, fanatical person. I save things. I'm a pack rat, I suppose. And if I want to save it, I will take very good care of it and be sure that I have it. And I was lucky. Nobody took this away from me. Because everything else was taken. So whenever I have anything that connects me to my youth, I hold on to if for dear life, because most of it was stolen from me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a sort of an entitlement document for things. There are various things. There's a suitcase listed here, shoes, a sweater, a coat, a ladies' summer dress, a ladies' wool dress. Oh, I don't know what this is. A suit, a skirt and a jacket. As you can see, none of these coupons have ever been used, because we never got anything for anything. What we usually got for this thing was sometimes we could get some margarine or some sugar, or-- I don't know what. Maybe a washcloth. I don't know. I never got anything for anything. Because 90% of the coupon-- I had this for five years-- or for two years, I mean. And most of the coupons are still on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who issued this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this was issued by the Jewish self-government, which is a joke. They were appointed, and they-- they issued this. They issued these kind of things. They issued money. They issued theater tickets. They were trying-- I don't know what, whether it was morbidity or-- or-- or-- or cynicism, or something. They made us want to think that we were living in a normal world, where things happened. Which was, of course, not true, and of course, did not happen. There was absolutely nothing except a sack of straw, a wooden pallet, and three-- three layers and the food that they gave you, whatever they called it, twice a day. The rest, you had to scrounge for. If they took your stuff away from you when you came, you had to starve to buy yourself something to wear. They didn't give you anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is one of the other ironies of my past. This is a sit-- sit-- seat for a concert in-- in a coffee house which was the-- the conductor was somebody named Taube, which was a very well-known Czech conductor. And they played often, [? Bach. ?] And we-- they-- they-- we had a concert. It might have been, very easily, during the time where-- while the Red Cross was inspecting the-- the ghetto. I don't know. That is-- that is a seat. We had to buy this with the-- with the money that was allowed to us for the work we were doing. I don't know, because It had nothing to do with any reason. Somebody felt like giving you 20 krone, so they gave you 20 krone. It had nothing-- you didn't get it on a regular basis. And it didn't-- it didn't buy anything except this nonsense. And it wasn't money. It was just-- I don't know. This is-- these are some of the money that we had in Theresienstadt. This was in krone, which was the-- the nomination of the Czech currency. And they came in from 1 to 100. And this money was used. If you wanted-- you could take all the mustard you wanted. But if you wanted it in a container rather than into your container if you didn't have a container, you had to pay with this money. You could go into a place which was called the delicatessen, and you could buy it. And if they gave it to you, in addition, you could get it in there. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this money was supposed to be used to buy tickets or theater tickets or-- I don't know. Sometimes I think you had to pay-- you had to pay postage due when you got the package from somebody, like food from somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: That if some people had somebody outside that was sending them packages, you had to pay for the package. And that's what they used this money for. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It came from 1 to 100 krone. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Oh yeah, when you went to the concert, you could buy coffee, what they called coffee. It was some sort of a brownish liquid. And they would charge you, I don't know, a few krone. I don't remember what. I never drank it, so I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is a picture of my brother, Henry, who now lives in Israel. This is taken at the time in 1945, when we were coming out of-- when we were coming out of Theresienstadt. He was 15 years old at the time. He looks like a little baby. He was very small. He started to grow afterwards. I guess all the malnutrition or something, stopped us all. I grew two inches after I was married, in 1948. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of me taking in the early fall of 1946, with a friend in a-- outside a-- a play-- a football field or something, in a Bavarian DP camp. It was not where I lived. I see that I have-- I had this coat made to-- made to order. I bought the cloth. And I felt very luxurious. Because I had something that wasn't a hand-me-down. It was something that was made for me, and that nobody else wore before. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm the one on the left, the shorter one. This is my sister-- cousin, Mira, the one that now lives in Australia, that lived in England for-- from 1942 till 1970. 1989, what am I talking about? This is her in 1946, with the-- the first two of her children. Do you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of them is called [? Jeffrey, ?] and one of them is called [? Glenn. ?] They were both boys. And then she had in 1956, she had two more-- a boy and a girl, June and Maurice. Maurice was named after her father. This is in front of her house. This is a picture of me, in spring of 1949. We went to visit somebody, and he was a-- he was a photographer, an uncle. And he was a-- a hobby photographer, so he took a picture of me. This is my passport, it says so, when I was coming to the United States of America. Since we didn't have any papers, this was issued to me in Munich. And this is what I came into the United States with in February 17, 1947. [PAUSES FOR 33 SECONDS] This is my ID card that I received for the Hebrew Immigrant Aid Society, HIAS, the people that brought me over and paid for my ticket. Because we didn't have any money. And somebody had to pay for the ship passage. And this is the name of the boat I was-- [? that ?] [? this ?] [? is ?] called [? Ernie ?] [? Pyle. ?] And it is stamped February 18, 1947. And this is the date that I was-- that-- that I received my visa. And this is sort of in together with the other one. One was issued by the American consulate in Munich, and this was issued by-- by somebody from the Hebrew [INAUDIBLE]. And it says that they should extend me courtesy, that they are responsible for me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of my father taken in New York, in about 1956. He died in 1957, when my son was five months old. I'm glad he lived to see at least one of his grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in August, 1948, of my first husband and me. We were on the honeymoon. And I had been told all my life that I had beautiful legs. And my husband said his were nicer than mine. And we took a picture of it. And we ask a friend of ours to judge. I won. This is a picture that was taken of all the survivors, plus one or two other additions, in 1950. The lady sitting on the left is-- is my father's older sister, [? Taeta ?]. The-- the young man behind her is my brother, Henry. The big man sitting next to her with the little boy on his-- on his knee is my Uncle Albert, her husband. The little boy on his knee is the only American in the picture. He was born in New York in 1947, in September. The woman on-- next, in the middle with the apron on is-- is my-- is the wife of my Uncle, Samuel, which is the-- the-- the one in the white shirt, sitting in the middle, the short one, who is my father's youngest brother. The lady in the dark blouse next to me is my father's second wife, whom he married in 1946. And that-- the gentleman sitting on the right is my father. And the other one is the older son. He was born in Deggendorf in 19-- in January, '46. He's-- the-- the couple in the middle, those are their two children. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my children a very long time ago. Gregory was born in '57. Pamela was born in 1960. This is taken approximately 19-- late 1961 or early 1962. They are now 38 and 35. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of me in an-- in my office. I would say it was in-- in-- I don't know, '78, '79, something like that. This is a picture of my father's two sisters. The one on the left side is the one that had been-- this is taken in Israel, in about 1972, early part of '72. The one on the left was the one that came to Israel in 1938. The one on the right came in 1971. Her husband had died. And she decided to go and live with her sister for awhile. And they hadn't seen one another for many, many years, 38 or whatever. And they couldn't live together anymore. So my aunt went back to England to be with her daughter. Well, I-- when-- the little one, I remember when I saw her the first time in Israel since 1938, when she left, I was a child and she was an adult. So when I came to see her in 1970, she was in an old-age home, where this is taken. And I went, and I wanted to put my arms around her and hugged her. And I closed my arms over my shoulders, over her head. Because I had grown, and she had shrunk. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] These are the two people, the two most important men in my life. The one in the green shirt is my son, Gregory. And the one on the left side is my second husband, Hyman. And he's a pearl too. And I'm glad I got him instead of the one I started out with. And my son, I'm very proud of. He's a wonderful person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm only sorry my daughter cannot be here, but she lives in Florida, and it was, unfortunately, not possible for her to-- to make it. OK, Greg, you want to say something? GREGORY (SON): Sure. Did you want to ask something? Or-- all right, I'll fire away. This-- I don't really have prepared remarks. So I guess I'm just going to be honest, which is always best.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm excited that my mother's doing this and has been able to do it to people that weren't really members of her family. I had done it once before and tried to get the most possible out of it, onto tape. Because my mother has a truly exciting story, which I'm sure she's told in great detail. It's a remarkable story, one of survival and of just incredible resourcefulness. It's one that wasn't really shared with me until I was already in college. There were bits and pieces of that history that would echo around our lives, but she never really spoke of it. And then I remember one night, finally hearing the entire account as it were, in-- you know, late at night. The lights were low. And she just told me the story. And I had no idea that any of these facts existed. And you know, as the years go by, it's-- I find out more and more details. And it's become, I guess, easier for her to talk about it and share with us. It's something where, you know, I really find I only have the greatest admiration for what she's been through and how she came out, and how we had this remarkably normal life in America. And you know, she's been through hell and came through hell to get us here. I'm glad that she's been able to tell her story. I missed a couple of times when she gave a speech. And so I never really saw her tell it. You know, in her desire to say, bring it to others and educate people for the future. And I'm just happy that it's really been recorded exhaustively, and well-researched. And really, it's been a goal of mine to someday try to write a book or make a video. But I think I've been so close to it that I've never gotten around to it. So clearly, at this point a tremendous step's been taken. And I'm very happy about that. And I'm very proud of her. I'll find myself a ghostwriter, and I'll dictate my story. And I'll give him all the credit, as long as it gets out and my name is spelled correctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll just make sure of that. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you like to say something, Hy? Are you happy living here? [LAUGHS] HYMAN (HUSBAND): I'm happily married, and I'm glad I'm here. All right. That's about as much as we can expect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. GREGORY (SON): Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
